feature1_escape_value.json.description: Escape an arbitrary string so it can be safely embedded inside an LDAP distinguished name or search filter. A mode selects which reserved set to neutralize: "all" escapes every byte, "dn" escapes only characters reserved within a distinguished name, "filter" escapes only characters reserved within a search filter, and "both" escapes the union of the two sets. An optional set of characters may be marked as ignored so they pass through unescaped. Each escaped byte is rendered as a backslash followed by its two lowercase hexadecimal digits; unescaped bytes are emitted verbatim. The output is the resulting escaped string.

feature2_1_single_conditions.json.description: Compile a single attribute condition into an LDAP search filter. A condition names an attribute, a match kind, and (for value-bearing matches) a value. The output reports two lines: "query=" carries the wire filter with the value escaped byte-by-byte for transmission, and "unescaped=" carries the same filter with values shown in their readable form. Equality wraps as (attr=value); negated equality wraps as (!(attr=value)); approximate, greater-or-equal and less-or-equal use ~=, >= and <=; presence tests use (attr=*) and its negation (!(attr=*)); substring matches place a wildcard before, after, or on both sides of the value, each with a negated variant. When no condition is supplied a default presence filter on the object-class attribute is produced. Attribute names containing reserved characters are themselves escaped.

feature2_2_combining_conditions.json.description: Combine multiple conditions into one filter. Conditions default to conjunction; a condition may instead be tagged as disjunctive. Two or more conjunctive conditions are wrapped in an AND group (&...). Two or more disjunctive conditions are wrapped in an OR group (|...). A lone disjunctive condition collapses to a single bare condition with no surrounding group. When exactly one conjunctive condition is mixed with exactly one disjunctive condition, the whole query becomes a single OR group of the two. When several conjunctive conditions coexist with several disjunctive ones, the conjunctive conditions sit at the top level of an AND group and the disjunctive ones are nested together inside an OR group within it.

feature2_3_set_and_range.json.description: Build convenience filters over a single attribute. A set-membership request lists candidate values for one attribute and produces a disjunction matching any of them: an OR group of equality conditions, one per value. A range request gives a lower and upper bound for one attribute and produces a conjunction of a greater-or-equal condition and a less-or-equal condition. Output reports the escaped wire filter and its unescaped readable form.

feature2_4_nested_and_raw.json.description: Compose nested filter groups and inject pre-built raw filter fragments. A group request wraps a set of inner conditions inside a single logical operator: "all" yields an AND group, "any" yields an OR group, and "none" yields a NOT group. Groups may be combined with each other and with top-level conditions, producing properly nested wire output. A raw fragment is inserted verbatim with no escaping, and raw fragments combine with ordinary conditions following the same conjunction/disjunction wrapping rules. Output reports the escaped wire filter and its unescaped readable form.

feature3_1_dn_decompose.json.description: Decompose a distinguished name into its parts. The output reports, as JSON arrays: the components (each "attribute=value" pair, with values still escaped), the relative distinguished names, the bare values in order, a list of [attribute, value] pairs, and an associative grouping mapping each lowercased attribute to the list of its values (case-insensitive merge). It also reports four scalar views: the value of the first (leftmost) pair, the attribute name of the first pair, the leftmost component as a string, and the parent (everything except the leftmost component). A malformed or non-distinguished-name input yields empty collections and empty scalar views.

feature3_2_dn_validate.json.description: Determine whether a string is a structurally valid distinguished name, and whether the normalized form is empty. A value is valid when it contains at least one well-formed attribute=value pair; bare words, an empty attribute, an empty value, or surrounding prose make it invalid. Whitespace around the attribute, the equals sign, and the whole string is tolerated. The output reports "valid=" and "empty=" as booleans, where an invalid value is always treated as empty.

feature3_3_dn_hierarchy.json.description: Compare two distinguished names for hierarchical relationships, matching attribute names and values case-insensitively. The output reports four booleans for the first name relative to the second: ancestor_of (the second is nested somewhere beneath the first), descendant_of (the first is nested somewhere beneath the second), parent_of (the second is exactly one level beneath the first), and child_of (the first is exactly one level beneath the second). Identical names are neither ancestor nor descendant of one another; empty or non-distinguished-name inputs yield all false.

feature4_dn_builder.json.description: Edit a distinguished name through an ordered sequence of immutable-style steps and read back the result. Steps: "reverse" flips the order of all components; "shift" removes a given count of components from the front; "pop" removes a given count from the end (removing more than present clears it); "append" adds a component at the end; "prepend" adds a component at the front. An added component may be given as an attribute plus a value, as a single "attribute=value" string, or as a list mixing both forms; surrounding whitespace on attribute and value is trimmed, and reserved characters in the value are escaped. The output reports the final distinguished name string.

feature5_parse_filter.json.description: Parse an LDAP search filter string into a node tree and reassemble it into canonical form. The output reports "nodes=", a JSON tree where each group node is {group: operator, nodes: [...]} and each leaf condition is {attribute, operator, value}, followed by "assembled=", the canonical filter rebuilt from that tree. Reassembly drops redundant surrounding parentheses and insignificant whitespace while preserving spaces inside values, and supports several sibling root nodes. A structurally invalid filter (unbalanced parentheses, an unclosed nested group, or a malformed condition) is reported as a single neutral error line "error=parser_exception".
